title: US ERISA Fiduciary Duty Notice
content:
MERIDIAN WEALTH PARTNERS - INTERNAL COMPLIANCE NOTICE

This notice serves to inform all advisors and senior staff of Meridian Wealth Partners' obligations and procedures regarding ERISA fiduciary duties when providing investment advice to employee benefit plans and individual retirement accounts.

FIDUCIARY STATUS UNDER ERISA SECTION 404

Meridian Wealth Partners acknowledges its fiduciary status under ERISA Section 404(a)(1) when providing investment advice to qualified retirement plans, 401(k) plans, and IRAs. As a fiduciary, the firm is held to the highest standard of care and must act solely in the interest of plan participants and beneficiaries. This fiduciary obligation extends to all investment recommendations, asset allocation strategies, and fee disclosures for retirement plan assets totaling approximately $847 million under management as of our last compliance review.

DOL FIDUCIARY RULE COMPLIANCE

Under the Department of Labor Fiduciary Rule, Meridian Wealth Partners must provide investment advice that meets the "prudent expert" standard. This requires that all recommendations be made with the care, skill, prudence, and diligence that a prudent person acting in a like capacity would use. When recommending proprietary products or third-party investments that generate additional compensation, advisors must ensure such recommendations satisfy the Best Interest Contract Exemption (BICE) requirements.

Key compliance requirements include:
- Documentation of all investment rationale with specific performance metrics and risk assessments
- Disclosure of all direct and indirect compensation exceeding 0.25% annually
- Quarterly monitoring of recommended investments with performance benchmarking against appropriate indices
- Maintenance of detailed records supporting the prudence of investment selections

FEE DISCLOSURE OBLIGATIONS

All ERISA-covered accounts require comprehensive fee disclosure including management fees (typically ranging from 0.85% to 1.45% annually), underlying fund expenses, trading costs, and any third-party service provider fees. For plans with assets exceeding $5 million, additional institutional pricing may apply, potentially reducing overall fee structures by 15-25 basis points.

Advisors must provide written disclosure of:
- Annual advisory fees and billing methodology
- Expense ratios of recommended mutual funds and ETFs
- Any 12b-1 fees, sales charges, or surrender charges
- Revenue sharing arrangements with fund companies
- Potential conflicts of interest related to proprietary products

PROHIBITED TRANSACTIONS

ERISA Section 404 strictly prohibits certain transactions between fiduciaries and plan assets. These include self-dealing, receiving kickbacks or undisclosed compensation, and using plan assets for the benefit of parties other than participants. Violations can result in personal liability for losses and disgorgement of profits.

DOCUMENTATION REQUIREMENTS

All ERISA-related advice must be documented in our compliance management system within 48 hours of client interaction. Documentation must include investment rationale, risk assessment, fee analysis, and confirmation that recommendations align with the plan's investment policy statement.

For rollovers from employer-sponsored plans to IRAs, advisors must provide written analysis demonstrating why the rollover serves the participant's best interest, including comparison of fees, investment options, services, and withdrawal flexibility.

This notice supplements our comprehensive ERISA compliance procedures outlined in Policy Manual Section 12.3. Questions regarding fiduciary obligations should be directed to the Compliance Department immediately.

Advisors violating ERISA fiduciary standards face potential regulatory sanctions, personal liability, and immediate disciplinary action up to and including termination.
